coredata.dc:title: Economical, green route to highly fluorescence intensity carbon materials based on ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots composites: Application as excellent fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Fe3+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Lina
Mao, Wei
Huang, Jinrui
Li, Shouhai
Huang, Kun
Li, Mei
Xia, Jianling
Chen, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: A facile, economical, and green method has been developed to prepare a new fluorescent sensor based on ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots (SL/GQDs) core/shell hybrids for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. The combination of these two materials brings both excellent photoluminescent properties (the fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs composites is four times higher than the free GQDs) and nice selectivity. In this composite, SL molecule plays two key roles in the system as a π-rich and sulfur-incorporated compound to enhance the fluorescence and as a chelator to detect Fe3+. The obtained sensor presents excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ and exhibits a good linearity in the range of 0.005–500μM with a detection limit as low as 0.5nM. We have further explored the practical use of such proposed probe for Fe3+ determination in lake water samples, which shows promise in environmental determination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignosulfonate
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Ferric ion
π–π interactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the SL/GQDs composites and GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31170544 , 51272100 ), the Changzhou Science and Technology Research Project and Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A low-cost, green route to construct a novel sensing platform for Fe3+ detection based on core/shell hybrids by using citric acid and sodium lignosulfonate through one-step treatment.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of SL (2.25g/L), GQDs (10.61g/L), SL/GQDs (12.42g/L) and SL/GQDs-Fe3+. (B) The excitation (a, c) and emission (b, d) fluorescence spectra of the GQDs (10.61g/L) and SL/GQDs (12.42g/L).
FT-IR spectra (A) of the SL, GQDs and SL/GQDs. The TEM images of (B) GQDs, (C) SL and (D) SL/GQDs (inset: the corresponding size distribution of SL/GQDs).

PL emission spectra (A) of SL/GQDs dispersion in the absence (curve a) and presence (curve b) of 50μM Fe3+ ions. PL emission spectra (B) of SL/GQDs dispersion in the presence of increasing Fe3+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.005, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, and 500μM).
Stern–Volmer plot of Fe3+ obtained with SL/GQDs (excitation at 362nm; F 0 and F are SL/GQDs fluorescence intensities at 462nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively).

Quenching performance of the SL/GQDs solution in the absence and presence of various metal ions ([M n+]=50μM, F 0 and F correspond to SL/GQDs fluorescence intensities at 442nm in the absence and presence of ions, respectively).
The difference in PL intensity of SL/GQDs dispersion under various conditions ([Fe3+]=50μM; [Cu2+]=[Zn2+]=[Ca2+]=5mM).

(a) PL spectra of SL/GQDs dispersion in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8μM) in lake water. (b) The dependence of (F 0/F)−1 on the concentrations of Fe3+ within the range of 0–8μM.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: N-GQDs were synthesized using ammonia as a nitrogen source.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GQDs were synthesized using a SBA-15 template as a nanoreactor via a HNO3 vapor cutting strategy.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: NCDs were simply prepared using ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: N-GQD was obtained by using a MOF-derived carbon (ZIF-8C) as a carbon source.

head.ce:title: Economical, green route to highly fluorescence intensity carbon materials based on ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots composites: Application as excellent fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomass Energy and Material, Jiangsu Province; Key and Open Laboratory on Forest Chemical Engineering, State Forestry Administration; National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Nanjing 210042, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomass Energy and Material, Jiangsu Province; Key and Open Laboratory on Forest Chemical Engineering, State Forestry Administration; National Engineering Laboratory for Biomass Chemical Utilization, Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, Chinese Academy of Forestry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A low-cost, green route to construct a novel sensing platform for Fe3+ detection based on core/shell hybrids by using citric acid and sodium lignosulfonate through one-step treatment.

A facile, economical, and green method has been developed to prepare a new fluorescent sensor based on ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots (SL/GQDs) core/shell hybrids for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. The combination of these two materials brings both excellent photoluminescent properties (the fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs composites is four times higher than the free GQDs) and nice selectivity. In this composite, SL molecule plays two key roles in the system as a π-rich and sulfur-incorporated compound to enhance the fluorescence and as a chelator to detect Fe3+. The obtained sensor presents excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ and exhibits a good linearity in the range of 0.005–500μM with a detection limit as low as 0.5nM. We have further explored the practical use of such proposed probe for Fe3+ determination in lake water samples, which shows promise in environmental determination.

ce:section.ce:para: Ferric ion (Fe3+) is an essential transition metal ion in most organisms and plays vital roles in biological, environmental, and chemical systems [1,2]. The deficiency or overload of iron may lead to several disorders and diseases such as anemia and Parkinson's disease [3,4]. Meanwhile, industrial and other anthropogenic processes have been constantly releasing heavy-metal ions into the environment raising serious environmental and health concerns during the past decades, among which ferric ion is one of the most dangerous and ubiquitous pollutants. Therefore, the selective detection of Fe3+ ion concentration in environmental and biological samples is considerable significant.

In recent years, fluorescent sensors for the detection of metal ions have several advantages over other conventional techniques, such as fast analysis, high sensitivity, and being nonsample-destructing [5,6]. Up to now, many fluorescent probes including organic dyes, metal nanoclusters, conjugated polymers, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), etc. have been developed for the detection of heavy metal ions [7–9]. However, these reported fluorescent materials have intrinsic defects such as heavy metals potential toxicity or complex synthesis routes, leading to environmental pollution and high-cost, which restricts tremendously the probes widespread application. Therefore, the development of alternatives with eco-friendliness and low-cost is highly demanded. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as the newest class of carbon nanomaterials, have received tremendous attention due to their unique and novel properties which include low toxicity, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence, tunable surface functionalities and easy preparation [10–13]. Therefore, great efforts have been focused on the preparation of GQDs and developing GQDs for applications as a new platform in detection systems [14–16]. Several successful examples have been reported to determine some ions (such as Pb2+, Hg2+, and PO4 3−) through fluorescence changes of GQDs [17–19]. However, few reports indicated that carbon nanomaterials with relatively low fluorescence intensity acted as a component of a sensitive sensing strategy, while the traditional surface functionalization process of nanomaterials usually involves time-consuming multiple steps.

Lignin as a renewable material is by far the most abundant biomass in nature. Ligninsulfonates are byproducts of the sulfite-pulping procedure, using several salts of sulfurous acid to extract lignin from wood [20]. Ligninsulfonates represent a significantly under-utilized biomass [21]. Although obtained millions of tons per year, the existing markets for ligninsulfonate products remain limited and focus on low value products such as dispersing or binding agents [22]. Thus, the development of the ligninsulfonates application has great research significance. Ligninsulfonates macromolecule contains a long hydrophobic backbone (C6–C3 structure moieties linked together by ether and carbon bonds) branched with hydrophilic side chains (carboxyl, sulfonic, and phenolic hydroxyl groups), and thus possesses a certain degree of surface activity and a remarkable affinity for some metal ions [23–27]. In this paper, we prepared SL/GQDs composites by uniform modification the GQDs with sodium lignosulfonate (SL) via the GQDs–SL interactions due to possible π–π stacking in a green and facile preparative route. It suggests that the existence of SL at the edge of GQDs may lead to enhancement of fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Moreover, abundant carboxyl and phenolic groups at the surface of GQDs may assist the interaction between SL and the metal ion, thus generating the SL/GQDs composites with both enhanced fluorescent properties and determination ability, as shown in Scheme 1 . Of particular relevance to this method is using renewable raw material to synthesis SL/GQDs composites by environmentally friendly one-step facile process, and thus is of significance in large-scale production of high fluorescent intensity carbon nanoparticles.

Sodium lignosulfonate (SL) were purchased from Aladdin chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). Citric acid monohydrate (AR) and NaOH (AR) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All other reagents used in this paper were analytical grade and used directly without further treatment. The water used throughout in this work was purified through redistillation process. Inorganic salts such as MnCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, NaNO3, KNO3, FeCl3, CoCl2, Mg(OAc)2, CuCl2, CdCl2, NiCl2 and AgNO3 were dissolved in double distilled water (100mL) to afford 50μmol/L aqueous solutions. All samples were prepared at room temperature. Lake water samples were obtained from the Xuanwu Lake of Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, China. The lake water samples were filtered through a 0.22μm membrane and then centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 10min before use.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out on an LS SS Perkin Elmer spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were measured by a Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were carried out on a Shimadzu UV-2550 PC spectrometer. The morphologies of the composites were examined by a Technai G2 20 S-Twin TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the SL/GQDs composites and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: SL/GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid (CA) through one step hydrothermal treatment to SL/NaOH system. In a typical synthesis, SL (0.09g) was dissolved in water (40g). CA monohydrate (2g, 9.52mmol) was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle for 15min until the CA changed to a light yellow liquid. Then the obtained liquid was transferred into 100mL of 10mg/mL NaOH solution in the presence of 200μL of SL dispersion. After stirring for 2h, the solution was neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH. For the synthesis of GQDs, a similar process was carried out but without the addition of SL dispersion. The GQDs solution was extensively dialyzed against abundant redistilled water in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 2d to obtain pristine GQDs. By freeze-dried method, white powder was obtained for further characterization.

The detection of Fe3+ ions was performed at room temperature in PBS (0.1M, 7.0) buffer solution. In a typical run, SL/GQDs powder was added into PBS buffer, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded after reaction for 20min at room temperature. The PL spectra of other metal ion stock solutions were also conducted in a similar way to confirm the selectivity for Fe3+. The selectivity and sensitivity measurements were conducted in triplicate to improve the statistics.

The preparation of SL/GQDs was completed by employing CA as the raw material to synthesize GQDs via one step hydrothermal treatment in the presence of SL. The obtained composites were dispersed well in water and remained stable for several months. The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of composites. The fluorescence quantum yield (excited at 362nm) of the SL/GQDs was 23.3% with quinine sulfate as a standard, which is much higher than the FLQY values of CQDs (10.9%). The extinction coefficient of SL/GQDs is 0.03Lg−1 cm−1. As seen from Fig. 1A, the GQDs and SL/GQDs exhibit a well-defined absorption band at 355nm with a narrow full width, which reflects that the sp2 clusters contained in GQDs and SL/GQDs should be uniform in size. On the other hand, the UV–vis absorption peak of the SL/GQDs-Fe3+ shows a significant increase with respect to that of SL/GQDs. This result reveals that Fe3+ ions have strong interaction with SL/GQDs affecting the absorption properties of GQDs. Moreover, the wavelength of the excitation peak of GQDs or SL/GQDs is almost the same as that of the emission peak of GQDs or SL/GQDs (Fig. 1B) suggesting that little non-emitting absorption has been generated in the GQDs or SL/GQDs. However, except for emitting blue light (462nm) when excited with 362nm UV beam, there are some differences in optical properties between the GQDs and the SL/GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs is four times higher than the free GQDs. The high fluorescence intensity of the prepared SL/GQDs means that the surfaces of the CQDs are well passivated, since studies have been shown that the fluorescence emission of CQDs without passivation is very weak but CQDs can exhibit strong fluorescence activities after appropriate surface passivation treatments [28,29]. As we know, incorporate sulfur atoms and π–π interaction play the key role in affecting the fluorescence activities [30,31]. Additionally, the SL microspheres probably favor collecting the incompletely carbonized citric acid at the edge of composites through π–π interaction, which could make GQDs surface-passivated [28,32]. Moreover, the fluorescence activity of the SL/GQDs was found to be pH-dependent (Fig. S2). The SL/GQDs show good fluorescence activities in neutral and alkaline conditions, while relatively low in acidic solutions. Probably, the pH-dependent FL property found for the SL/GQDs may be due to the protonization of the sulfonic groups at their surfaces, which is negatively charged above pH 6. Therefore, the FL activities increase with decreasing pH due to their electrostatic repulsion [28]. Therefore we can prepare a new fluorescent sensor by making use of the high sensitivity of conjugated composites to external structural perturbations and electron density changes of the composites backbone, when the composites interact and form complexes with the analyte.

The preparation of SL/GQDs was completed by employing CA as the raw material to synthesize GQDs via one step hydrothermal treatment in the presence of SL. The obtained composites were dispersed well in water and remained stable for several months. The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were carried out to investigate the optical properties of composites. The fluorescence quantum yield (excited at 362nm) of the SL/GQDs was 23.3% with quinine sulfate as a standard, which is much higher than the FLQY values of CQDs (10.9%). The extinction coefficient of SL/GQDs is 0.03Lg−1 cm−1. As seen from Fig. 1A, the GQDs and SL/GQDs exhibit a well-defined absorption band at 355nm with a narrow full width, which reflects that the sp2 clusters contained in GQDs and SL/GQDs should be uniform in size. On the other hand, the UV–vis absorption peak of the SL/GQDs-Fe3+ shows a significant increase with respect to that of SL/GQDs. This result reveals that Fe3+ ions have strong interaction with SL/GQDs affecting the absorption properties of GQDs. Moreover, the wavelength of the excitation peak of GQDs or SL/GQDs is almost the same as that of the emission peak of GQDs or SL/GQDs (Fig. 1B) suggesting that little non-emitting absorption has been generated in the GQDs or SL/GQDs. However, except for emitting blue light (462nm) when excited with 362nm UV beam, there are some differences in optical properties between the GQDs and the SL/GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs is four times higher than the free GQDs. The high fluorescence intensity of the prepared SL/GQDs means that the surfaces of the CQDs are well passivated, since studies have been shown that the fluorescence emission of CQDs without passivation is very weak but CQDs can exhibit strong fluorescence activities after appropriate surface passivation treatments [28,29]. As we know, incorporate sulfur atoms and π–π interaction play the key role in affecting the fluorescence activities [30,31]. Additionally, the SL microspheres probably favor collecting the incompletely carbonized citric acid at the edge of composites through π–π interaction, which could make GQDs surface-passivated [28,32]. Moreover, the fluorescence activity of the SL/GQDs was found to be pH-dependent (Fig. S2). The SL/GQDs show good fluorescence activities in neutral and alkaline conditions, while relatively low in acidic solutions. Probably, the pH-dependent FL property found for the SL/GQDs may be due to the protonization of the sulfonic groups at their surfaces, which is negatively charged above pH 6. Therefore, the FL activities increase with decreasing pH due to their electrostatic repulsion [28]. Therefore we can prepare a new fluorescent sensor by making use of the high sensitivity of conjugated composites to external structural perturbations and electron density changes of the composites backbone, when the composites interact and form complexes with the analyte.

FT-IR spectra analysis was recorded to identify the construction of composites. As shown in Fig. 2A, GQDs exhibit characteristic peaks at 1603 and 1401cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of CO and the deformation of CO in the carboxyl group, respectively. Additionally, the broad absorption band centered at 3436cm−1 can be attributed to the hydroxyl groups linked to the GQDs. SL/GQDs composites also exhibit these characteristic peak, but the functional group peak has a little shifts because of interaction between GQDs and SL. A noticeable difference between GQDs and SL/GQDs lies in the new absorption band centered at 1189 and 1129cm−1, corresponded to the stretching vibration of SO in the sulfonate group of SL, which indicates composites have formed successfully. Moreover, the morphology and structure of composites were presented by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 2B shows GQDs display fairly monodisperse nanoparticles of near spherical morphology distributed in the range of 2–8nm. TEM micrograph of SL samples is presented in Fig. 2C, which displays that SL agglomerate together and form clusters. Fig. 2D shows that SL/GQDs composites are presented distinct core/shell morphology, which SL fold into microspheres as GQDs screen the intramolecular electrostatic repulsion of SL and GQDs are present at the periphery of SL microspheres. The diameters of the SL/GQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 390–800nm with an average diameter of 0.59μm (the inset of Fig. 2D).

We first investigated the sensitivity and feasibility of using such SL/GQDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ by titration of the SL/GQDs with Fe3+ in phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). Fig. 3 shows the PL spectra of SL/GQDs dispersion under various conditions. It is seen that the solution of SL/GQDs in the absence of metal ions exhibited a strong PL peak at 462nm (Fig. 3A, curve a). In contrast, the presence of Fe3+ leads to an obvious decrease in the fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs, indicating that Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of SL/GQDs (Fig. 3A, curve b). As shown in Fig. 3B, the concentration dependent fluorescence measurements of SL/GQDs with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–500μM were monitored to prove the sensitivity of Fe3+ ions detection, indicating that the PL intensity of the composites is sensitive to Fe3+ concentration and decreases gradually with an increased Fe3+ concentration. As suggested by previous reports, the above phenomenon can be attributed to that Fe3+ ions could form metal hydroxides by specially coordinating with the abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups of SL/GQDs. [33] Besides, the sulfonate and carboxyl groups on the surface and edge of SL/GQDs may also be involved in Fe3+ detecting [34]. Based on the above analyses, a coordinated Fe-SL/GQDs complex would be formed, which may facilitate charge transfer from SL/GQDs to Fe3+ and thus lead to a significant fluorescence quenching behavior.

To further research about the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs, the fluorescence quenching data of SL/GQDs toward Fe3+ were analyzed by the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
sv
c
where F 0 and F are the PL intensities of SL/GQDs at 460nm in the absence and presence of a quencher (Fe3+ is the quencher in this study), respectively, K sv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and c is the analyte (Fe3+) concentration. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 4 fits a good linear correlation (R 2 =0.997) over the concentration range of 0.005–500μM. From the formula, we calculated the quenching constant is 0.004. The detection limit is about 0.5nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is much lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency allowed maximum tolerance (5.4μM) in drinking water [35]. The possible PL quenching mechanism can be interpreted by the fluorescence quenching spectra of the Stern–Volmer curves of SL/GQDs with Fe3+ at different temperatures (30°C, 50°C) as shown in Fig. S1. It can be found from Fig. S1 that the Stern–Volmer plots are linear and the quenching constant decrease with increasing temperature, which indicates the static quenching interaction between Fe3+ and SL/GQDs. As shown in Table 1 , the results of SL/GQDs sensor for detecting Fe3+ are compared with that of other reported quantum dots-based sensors, which suggests that the prepared sensor system demonstrates superior sensitivity than previously reported sensing systems over a wide concentration range.

To further research about the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs, the fluorescence quenching data of SL/GQDs toward Fe3+ were analyzed by the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
sv
c
where F 0 and F are the PL intensities of SL/GQDs at 460nm in the absence and presence of a quencher (Fe3+ is the quencher in this study), respectively, K sv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and c is the analyte (Fe3+) concentration. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 4 fits a good linear correlation (R 2 =0.997) over the concentration range of 0.005–500μM. From the formula, we calculated the quenching constant is 0.004. The detection limit is about 0.5nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is much lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency allowed maximum tolerance (5.4μM) in drinking water [35]. The possible PL quenching mechanism can be interpreted by the fluorescence quenching spectra of the Stern–Volmer curves of SL/GQDs with Fe3+ at different temperatures (30°C, 50°C) as shown in Fig. S1. It can be found from Fig. S1 that the Stern–Volmer plots are linear and the quenching constant decrease with increasing temperature, which indicates the static quenching interaction between Fe3+ and SL/GQDs. As shown in Table 1, the results of SL/GQDs sensor for detecting Fe3+ are compared with that of other reported quantum dots-based sensors, which suggests that the prepared sensor system demonstrates superior sensitivity than previously reported sensing systems over a wide concentration range.

In order to assess the specificity of the obtained SL/GQDs sensor for detection Fe3+, we carried out PL measurements in the presence of potential interfering metal ions such as Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ under the same concentration and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . It is seen that SL/GQDs with Fe3+ system has the strongest fluorescence quenching efficiency, whereas no apparent fluorescence signal changes are observed by the addition of other metal ions into the SL/GQDs dispersion. The selectivity of Fe3+ can be evaluated by the selectivity coefficient (KFe3+ = SFe3+/S 0), where SFe3+ is the response to Fe3+ and S 0 is the response to any other cation [39]. The selectivity coefficients data are listed in Table S1. It is evident from Fig. 5 and Table S1 that the sensor has good selectivity for Fe3+ over other ions. To investigate the effectiveness of SL/GQDs, we also performed Fe3+ detection in the presence of 100-fold Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+ or Mg2+ as competing metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 , the quenching response is not significantly affected by the interfering metal ions. The high selectivity of the proposed sensor for Fe3+ is probably due to that Fe3+ has higher thermodynamic affinity and faster chelating process with SL/GQDs than other metal ions. Thus, a coordinated Fe-SL/GQDs complex would be probably formed, which would facilitate charge transfer and restrain the recombination of excitons, leading to the PL quenching behavior. The results imply that the developed novel fluorescent sensing platform might be promising for the determination of Fe3+ in aqueous media.

In order to assess the specificity of the obtained SL/GQDs sensor for detection Fe3+, we carried out PL measurements in the presence of potential interfering metal ions such as Ag+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ under the same concentration and the results were shown in Fig. 5. It is seen that SL/GQDs with Fe3+ system has the strongest fluorescence quenching efficiency, whereas no apparent fluorescence signal changes are observed by the addition of other metal ions into the SL/GQDs dispersion. The selectivity of Fe3+ can be evaluated by the selectivity coefficient (KFe3+ = SFe3+/S 0), where SFe3+ is the response to Fe3+ and S 0 is the response to any other cation [39]. The selectivity coefficients data are listed in Table S1. It is evident from Fig. 5 and Table S1 that the sensor has good selectivity for Fe3+ over other ions. To investigate the effectiveness of SL/GQDs, we also performed Fe3+ detection in the presence of 100-fold Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+ or Mg2+ as competing metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6, the quenching response is not significantly affected by the interfering metal ions. The high selectivity of the proposed sensor for Fe3+ is probably due to that Fe3+ has higher thermodynamic affinity and faster chelating process with SL/GQDs than other metal ions. Thus, a coordinated Fe-SL/GQDs complex would be probably formed, which would facilitate charge transfer and restrain the recombination of excitons, leading to the PL quenching behavior. The results imply that the developed novel fluorescent sensing platform might be promising for the determination of Fe3+ in aqueous media.

To evaluate the feasibility of the obtained SL/GQDs for detecting Fe3+ in a real world context, the fluorescence experiments for real water sample analysis were then repeated using lake water samples obtained from the Xuanwu Lake of Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, China. The obtained water samples were used as diluted solvent instead of deionized water to spike with Fe3+ at different concentration levels. As shown in Fig. 7a, the PL intensity of SL/GQDs decreases with increased concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 8μM. The calibration curve of Fe3+ with SL/GQDs in lake water was obtained by plotting the values of (F 0/F)−1 versus the concentrations of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7b). Despite the interference from wide range of minerals and organics existing in lake water, the proposed sensing platform is still effective for distinguishing between fresh lake water and that doped 2μM Fe3+, exhibiting similar PL intensity changes to the experiments carried out in distilled water and satisfying the practical Fe3+ detection in real samples. These results indicate that the present Fe3+ probe is likely to be capable of the practical application in biological and environmental determination of Fe3+ detection upon further development.

In conclusion, we successfully constructed a novel sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+ ions based on core/shell hybrid composites by using citric acid and sodium lignosulfonate through one-step treatment for the first time. The combination of these two materials brings both excellent photoluminescent properties (the fluorescence intensity of SL/GQDs composites is four times higher than the free GQDs) and nice selectivity, which allows the proposed biosensor possessing high sensitivity and specificity toward Fe3+ analysis versus other metal ions. More importantly, this sensing platform has been used for the analysis of lake water sample successfully. It opens a novel route to the modification of nanomaterial with low fluorescence intensity substrates and the preparation of functional composite materials which can selectively identify a target by a simple, low-cost route. Additionally, with its availability in large quantities at relatively low price, lignin is expected to find applications in the development and mass production of such fluorescent sensor fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31170544, 51272100), the Changzhou Science and Technology Research Project and Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.12.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorogenic and chromogenic detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ species in aqueous media by rhodamine–triazole conjugate
A novel colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensor for acetate ions in aqueous media based on a rhodamine 6G–phenylurea conjugate in the presence of Fe(III) ions
Future of toxicology iron chelators and differing modes of action and toxicity: the changing face of iron chelation therapy
Brain iron uptake and homeostatic mechanisms: an overview
Mercaptoacetic acid capped CdS quantum dots as fluorescence single shot probe for mercury (II)
Luminescent CdS quantum dots as selective ion probes
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Facile synthesis of molecularly imprinted graphene quantum dots for the determination of dopamine with affinity-adjustable
A near-infrared fluorescent probe for detecting copper (II) with high selectivity and sensitivity and its biological imaging applications
Size-controlled soft-template synthesis of carbon nanodots toward versatile photoactive materials
A fluorescent turn-off/on method for detection of Cu2+ and oxalate using carbon dots as fluorescent probes in aqueous solution
Eco-friendly synthesis of size-controllable amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots with antimycoplasma properties
One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
A graphene quantum dot-based method for the highly sensitive and selective fluorescence turn on detection of biothiols
Graphene quantum dots/l-cysteine coreactant electrochemiluminescence system and its application in sensing lead (II) ions
Graphene quantum dots combined with europium ions as photoluminescent probes for phosphate sensing
Co-ordination network for lignin—standardisation, production and applications adapted to market requirements (EUROLIGNIN)
The catalytic valorization of lignin for the production of renewable chemicals
Adsorption of metal ions on lignin
Surface-active properties of hydrophobized derivatives of lignosulfonates: effect of structure of organosilicon modifier
A new model for the structure of lignosulphonates: Part 1. Behaviour in dilute solutions
Structural modification of commercial lignosulphonates through laccase catalysis and ozonolysis
Sulfur-incorporated carbon quantum dots with a strong long-wavelength absorption band
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Sulfur-incorporated carbon quantum dots with a strong long-wavelength absorption band
Dopamine fluorescent sensors based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots core/shell hybrids
One-pot synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ ions detection
Graphitic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions
Nanoreactor-confined synthesis and separation of yellow-luminescent graphene quantum dots with a recyclable SBA-15 template and their application for Fe(III) sensing
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots by doping nitrogen element and its application in the detection of Fe(III)
Fabrication of a nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot from MOF-derived porous carbon and its application for highly selective fluorescence detection of Fe3+
A novel ratiometric fluorescent Fe3+ sensor based on a phenanthroimidazole chromophore

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lina Xu received the Master degree in Chemistry in 2014 at Nanjing University. Since 2014 she is a member of Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, whose research is focussed on sensor and composites.
Wei Mao is a master student at Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products. Her current research interests include biomass and sensor.

Jinrui Huang received the PhD degree in Chemistry in 2014 at the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry. Since 2014 he is a Member of Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, whose research is focussed on composites.
Shouhai Li is an associate professor at Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products. His current research interests are in the field of biomass utilization and composites.
Kun Huang is a member of Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products, whose research is focussed on biomass utilization.
Mei Li is a member of Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products. Her current research interests are in the field of biomass utilization.
Jianling Xia is a professor at Institute of Chemical Industry of Forest Products. Her current research interests are in the field of sensor and biomass utilization.
Qiang Chen is a senior engineer at Nanjing University. His current research interests are in the field of biomaterials.
